genre: Jazz (bass — ECM, spiritual jazz, Standards Trio)

location.formative: U.S. Army (Germany) — switched from piano to bass; Westlake College of Music, Los Angeles; New York City — Bill Evans, Albert Ayler, Paul Bley, Miles Davis, Tony Williams (1960s); Japan — Zen Buddhism study (early 1970s)
location.career: New York City (1960s-1970s); Pacific Northwest / Seattle area; ECM Records artist (1977-2015+); Keith Jarrett Standards Trio (1983-2014)

theory_nodes.concept: Bass as harmonic foundation (overtone series, root motion defines chord identity)
examples: Gary Peacock's Standards Trio bass lines — redefining harmony through bass-note choice and inversion
Scott LaFaro with Bill Evans — the melodic bass revolution that preceded Peacock's work
Ray Brown with Oscar Peterson — traditional walking bass as canonical harmonic foundation
Charles Mingus — bass as compositional voice, the most architecturally ambitious bassist before the free era
theory_nodes.description: The bass register is the harmonic foundation of all Western music — not by convention but by physics. The overtone series is most clearly audible in the bass register: a vibrating bass string produces harmonics at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency (octave, fifth, double octave, major third, etc.). These overtones are the physical origin of consonance and dissonance. The lowest-sounding note in a texture functions as the perceived root, regardless of what other instruments play. The bassist has more harmonic power than any other ensemble member: by choosing a single note, the bassist defines the harmonic context for everyone. Peacock exploited this power through inversions and substitutions — playing chord tones other than the root to transform harmonic identity while the upper instruments played unchanged.

ancestors: Bass-line inflection in arranging (degree 0 — Jones as orchestral architect)
Piano left-hand as bass voice (degrees 5-6 — Berry stride, Bown hard bop voicings)
genealogy.relationship: Jones (degree 0) is the foundational voice of the engine — the arranger whose bass lines in orchestral charts defined harmonic motion. Peacock (degree 8) is the literal bass instrument, arriving to make that foundation explicit. The circle spirals back to its origin. Berry (degree 5) and Bown (degree 6) played piano left-hand bass lines; Peacock plays the actual bass. The harmonic foundation that was implicit in the piano's left hand becomes the dedicated voice of a single instrument.

theory_nodes.concept: Walking bass (quarter-note pulse creating harmonic rhythm and forward motion)
examples: Gary Peacock with Keith Jarrett Standards Trio — walking bass that dissolves into rubato and reconstitutes
Ray Brown with Oscar Peterson — the canonical walking bass, steady quarter-note propulsion
Paul Chambers with Miles Davis Kind of Blue — modal walking bass in static harmonic fields
Ron Carter with Miles Davis second quintet — rhythmically advanced walking bass with metric displacement
theory_nodes.description: Walking bass is the foundational rhythmic and harmonic technique of jazz bass: a continuous quarter-note line that simultaneously outlines chord changes (harmonic function), provides rhythmic momentum (swing/groove), and creates an independent melody (the best walking lines are musical phrases in their own right). Peacock transcended the walking format by treating it as one option among many — walking when the music needed propulsion, dissolving into rubato when it needed space, and re-establishing the walk when momentum was required. The ability to move in and out of walking bass separates a great bassist from a competent one.

genealogy.relationship: Walking bass is the rhythmic cousin of Berry's steady stride pulse (degree 5) — both provide metronomic quarter-note foundation. But walking bass adds harmonic content (the notes outline chord changes) while stride piano adds chordal voicings. Anderson's elastic swing (degree 3) is the vocal equivalent of Peacock's rubato departures from the walking pulse — both stretch time organically, breath-driven rather than clock-driven.

theory_nodes.concept: Counterpoint — melodic bass independence (Scott LaFaro tradition, bass as conversational voice)
examples: Scott LaFaro with Bill Evans Sunday at the Village Vanguard — the contrapuntal revolution in jazz bass
Gary Peacock with Keith Jarrett Standards Trio — counterpoint sustained over thirty years
Charles Mingus — bass as compositional voice, the most contrapuntal bassist before LaFaro
theory_nodes.description: Counterpoint is the combination of two or more independent melodic lines simultaneously. In jazz, the contrapuntal bass tradition was revolutionized by Scott LaFaro (with Bill Evans, late 1950s), who transformed the bass from a supporting harmonic-and-rhythmic role into a conversational melodic voice. Peacock played with Evans after LaFaro's death (1961) and carried the contrapuntal tradition forward into the Standards Trio, where his melodic bass lines created ongoing three-way counterpoint with Jarrett's piano and DeJohnette's drums. The contrapuntal bass requires real-time composition — creating an independent melody that is coherent alone while fitting the harmonic and rhythmic ensemble context.

ancestors: Piano-bass conversational equality — Bill Evans/Scott LaFaro model
genealogy.relationship: The contrapuntal bass extends the composition-improvisation spectrum by requiring the bassist to compose in real time. Previous degrees tracked composition (Jones, 0 — scored arrangement), improvisation (Frisell, 1 — open-form guitar), and collective free improvisation (Priester, 7 — Arkestra). Peacock adds contrapuntal real-time composition: the bassist creates an independent melody that simultaneously serves harmonic function, rhythmic function, and melodic conversation. This is the most demanding form of jazz bass playing.

theory_nodes.concept: Zen Buddhism and music — ego dissolution, emptiness, presence as musical practice
examples: Gary Peacock's Standards Trio approach — 'the music plays itself through you'
Keith Jarrett solo concerts — extended improvisations that Jarrett describes in quasi-spiritual terms
John Coltrane's late period — spiritual jazz seeking transcendence through music (A Love Supreme, Ascension)
theory_nodes.description: Peacock's engagement with Zen Buddhism (including extended study in Japan in the early 1970s) was not incidental to his music — it was foundational. Three Zen concepts map directly to his bass practice: Emptiness (sunyata) — the bass creates harmonic gravity through implication rather than saturation, with spaces between notes as important as the notes themselves. Presence (mindfulness) — each note played with complete attention, no anticipation of the next, no memory of the last; the bass line unfolds in pure present tense. Ego dissolution — the bassist's conventional service role (support the soloist, be invisible) becomes a philosophical position: the bass IS the music not because it dominates but because it holds the space that makes everything else possible. The dissolution of ego is not self-erasure; it is recognition that the individual voice IS the collective voice when properly aligned.

ancestors: Sun Ra Afrofuturism — cosmic philosophy shaping musical practice (degree 7, via Priester)
Spiritual jazz tradition — Coltrane, Sanders, Ayler
genealogy.relationship: Priester (degree 7) brought Sun Ra's Afrofuturist philosophy — music as vehicle for cosmic liberation. Peacock (degree 8) brings Zen Buddhism — music as vehicle for ego dissolution and presence. Both are non-Western philosophical frameworks shaping musical practice, but they face different directions: Afrofuturism is expansive (music reaches outward toward the cosmos), Zen is contractive (music reaches inward toward emptiness). The S36 engine now has two philosophical-influence threads running in parallel.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note — bass register fundamental (the eighth deployment, returning to the root)
examples: Gary Peacock Standards Trio — bass inflection of blue notes in the lowest register
Ray Brown — walking bass blue-note bending through string technique
Charles Mingus — bass as blues voice, the most vocally expressive bassist in jazz
theory_nodes.description: The blue note thread returns to the bass register at degree 8 — the instrument whose physical register produces the strongest overtones and the most audible harmonic identity. The bass player's blue note is not a bend (like guitar or trombone) or a vocal inflection (like Anderson) but a choice of pitch placement within the quarter-tone zone between major and minor. On the upright bass (unfretted), the player's finger placement determines pitch continuously — like the trombone slide (degree 7, Priester) and the human voice (degree 3, Anderson). The bass blue note operates in the register where it has the most harmonic weight: a blue-noted bass tone changes the entire ensemble's harmonic color more powerfully than the same inflection in a higher register. Eight deployments across nine degrees: bass-line inflection (Jones, 0), upper-register guitar ambiguity (Frisell, 1), vocal melisma (Anderson, 3), trumpet vibrato zone (Standifer, 4), pianistic crush (Berry, 5), hard-bop altered voicings (Bown, 6), trombone slide continuum (Priester, 7), bass register fundamental (Peacock, 8).

ancestors: Blue note — all seven prior deployments
genealogy.relationship: Eight deployments across nine degrees. The blue note thread spirals back to the bass register at degree 8, echoing degree 0 (Jones — bass-line inflection in orchestral arranging). But where Jones's blue note was written into arrangements (composed), Peacock's blue note is played in real time (improvised). The upright bass is unfretted — like the trombone (degree 7) and the human voice (degree 3), it has continuous pitch access. The bass register gives the blue note its maximum harmonic weight.

seattle_connection: Drew to Pacific Northwest / Seattle area after decades of New York and international career. Quiet gravitational presence in the regional jazz community — not a scene-builder (like Frisell/Horvitz) or an institutional educator (like Priester/Standifer) but a foundational presence whose influence operated through proximity rather than construction. ECM Records artist connecting to Frisell (1) and Priester (7) — third S36 ECM artist. Energy-1 profile consistent with gravitational, non-self-promoting community presence. The Quiet Draw pattern: global cultivation, PNW settlement, presence without scene-building.

theory_genealogy: Bass as harmonic foundation — overtone series physics, root motion defines chord identity, bassist has more harmonic power than any other ensemble member through single-note choice
Walking bass — quarter-note pulse providing simultaneous harmonic function, rhythmic momentum, and melodic shape; Peacock transcended the form by moving in and out of walking freely
Counterpoint — melodic bass independence; Scott LaFaro revolution carried forward by Peacock into Standards Trio; real-time composition as the most demanding bass practice
Zen Buddhism and music — first non-Western philosophical framework in S36; ego dissolution, emptiness, presence as musical practice; maps directly to bass's ensemble role
Blue note — eighth deployment; bass register fundamental returns the thread to degree 0's territory; the register where blue notes carry maximum harmonic weight
immediate_items: Degree 9 should consider genre rotation. Nine consecutive jazz artists. The engine's subtitle is 'Seattle 360' — it must eventually encompass rock, grunge, hip-hop, classical, folk, electronic. The jazz cluster is now extraordinarily deep.
ECM label thread now three artists (Frisell 1, Priester 7, Peacock 8). ECM is becoming the engine's most significant label thread — aesthetic (space, texture, contemplation) as well as commercial.
Gender dimension: two women in nine degrees (Anderson 3, Bown 6). Seven men. CRITICAL tracking continues. Three consecutive male artists (7, 8, ...).
Zen thread established. Track all subsequent Eastern philosophical influences in S36 artists.
Energy-1 now represented. Track energy distribution as engine grows — currently skewed 1:4:4 (one energy-1, four energy-2, four energy-3).
Bass theory branch established with three nodes. Track all subsequent bass or bass-adjacent instruments.
Peacock's full ECM discography (leader and sideman) not completely cataloged. Complete in future.
Peacock-Evans recordings not fully documented. Cross-reference Bill Evans discography for Peacock credits.
Peacock-Ayler recordings not fully documented. Albert Ayler discography cross-reference needed.
Duwamish territorial acknowledgment still missing. NINTH RELEASE. BLOCKING. CRITICAL.
Berry recordings catalog — carried from degree 5.
Bown session discography — carried from degree 6.
Priester ECM discography — carried from degree 7.
Post-Jackson Street venue mapping — carried from degree 5.
pattern_items: Artist-city taxonomy: nine named patterns including Quiet Draw (degree 8). The energy-modified Draw. Classify every degree.

Composition-improvisation spectrum: Peacock adds contrapuntal real-time composition — the bassist composing an independent melody in real time while serving harmonic/rhythmic function. Seven positions now.
Rhythmic spectrum: seven positions (regular, polymetric, rubato, elastic swing, hard bop drive, electronic-spatial, breath-driven organic — Peacock). Continue tracking.
Chromaticism thread: eight deployments. Peacock adds bass-register chromaticism — chromatic passing tones in walking lines, chromatic approach notes. Continue tracking.
Blue note thread: eight deployments across nine degrees. Bass register fundamental returns the thread to degree 0 territory. Continue tracking.

Artist-as-institution spectrum: nine positions (industry, community, performer, presence, educator, persistence, erasure, bridge, seeker). Peacock adds The Seeker — withdraws from profession for philosophical reasons. Continue tracking.
Vocal theory branch: five nodes from degree 3. Extend at subsequent vocalist degrees.
Brass theory branch: three nodes across two degrees (Standifer/Priester). Extend at subsequent brass/wind degrees.
Piano theory branch: seven nodes across degrees 5-6. Still richest instrument branch. Extend at subsequent piano degrees.
Bass theory branch: three nodes at degree 8 (harmonic foundation, walking bass, counterpoint). NEW branch. Extend at subsequent bass degrees.
Body-as-sound-source thread: six instruments (voice, trumpet, piano-as-percussion, piano-as-percussive-comping, trombone-as-electroacoustic-source, bass-as-vibrating-string). Continue tracking.

Amiga Principle gradient: Peacock confirms the small-group mode (Standards Trio — Jarrett as leader, Peacock as voice within the trio). Four scales now: large ensemble/Jones, small group/Bown, solo/Berry, piano trio/Peacock.

Educational pipeline diversity: ten pipelines across nine degrees (Garfield, Berklee, UC Santa Cruz, UW, bandstand apprenticeship, self-taught, Seattle University, DuSable, Cornish, Westlake/LA). Peacock adds Westlake and Army-band pipeline.
Formal/informal pedagogy axis: three modes stable (preservation/Standifer, expansion/Priester, survival/Bown). Peacock is not a pedagogue — does not add a mode.
Gender-barrier gradient: tracking continues. Two women, seven men in nine degrees.
Session-pianist labor mode: three modes stable. Not applicable to bass.
Jackson Street corridor categories: Peacock is non-corridor (like Priester, degree 7). Classify all future artists.
Label threads: Mercury (degrees 0, 3). Columbia (degree 6). ECM (degrees 1, 7, 8 — now three artists, largest label cluster). Track all label connections.

Electronic jazz waves: three waves stable (proto-electronic/Sun Ra, first wave/Mwandishi, second wave/Frisell-Horvitz). Peacock is acoustic — does not add a wave but represents the acoustic-purity pole of the ECM aesthetic.
The Bridge category: Priester (degree 7). Peacock is not a bridge — he is a seeker. Different institutional category.
Zen thread: NEW. First non-Western philosophical framework. Track all subsequent Eastern philosophical influences.
Energy spectrum: complete range now represented (1-2-3). Distribution: 1:4:4. Track and note asymmetries.

key_albums.title: Tales of Another
key_albums.label: ECM
key_albums.significance: Trio with Keith Jarrett and Jack DeJohnette — the recording that established the chemistry which would become the Standards Trio. Peacock's compositions as the structural basis. Proto-Standards Trio: the three musicians discovering their conversational voice before the formal trio began.

key_albums.title: Standards, Vol. 1 (as sideman — Keith Jarrett)
key_albums.label: ECM
key_albums.significance: First official Standards Trio album. Establishes the trio's methodology: no rehearsal, collective reinterpretation of standards, radical listening. Peacock's bass provides the harmonic foundation while actively redefining the harmony through inversions and substitutions.

key_albums.title: Standards, Vol. 2 (as sideman — Keith Jarrett)
key_albums.label: ECM
key_albums.significance: Companion to Vol. 1, recorded at the same sessions. Together, the two volumes define the Standards Trio's first statement — the vocabulary that would be developed over the next three decades.

key_albums.title: Standards Live (as sideman — Keith Jarrett)
key_albums.label: ECM
key_albums.significance: Live recording showing the trio's concert methodology — unrehearsed, spontaneous selection of repertoire, real-time collective arrangement. Documents the trio at the point of total fluency.

key_albums.title: Now This
key_albums.label: ECM
key_albums.significance: Late-career leader recording with pianist Marc Copland. Peacock as composer and conceptual leader. Demonstrates that his compositional voice — not just his sideman bass — deserved sustained documentation.

degree_neighbors.name: Quincy Jones
degree_neighbors.relationship: Bass voice parallel — Jones at degree 0 is the foundational voice of the engine, the one who set the harmonic context. Peacock at degree 8 is the literal bass instrument arriving to make that foundation explicit. The circle spirals back. Jones's orchestral bass writing connects to Peacock's bass practice from the performer's side.

degree_neighbors.name: Bill Frisell
degree_neighbors.relationship: ECM label connection — both recorded extensively for ECM. Frisell's processed guitar and Peacock's acoustic bass are opposite timbral approaches within the same label aesthetic (space, texture, contemplation). Both embody quiet intensity (energy 2 and energy 1 respectively).

degree_neighbors.name: Julian Priester
degree_neighbors.relationship: ECM connection — three S36 ECM artists (Frisell, Priester, Peacock). Priester's Mwandishi electronic jazz and Peacock's Standards Trio acoustic jazz are two poles of the ECM aesthetic — electronic texture vs. acoustic purity. Both deeply influenced by non-Western philosophy (Afrofuturism vs. Zen).

degree_neighbors.name: Overton Berry
degree_neighbors.relationship: Piano-bass pairing — Berry the pianist, Peacock the bassist. In any jazz ensemble, piano and bass are the harmonic partnership that defines the chord progression. Berry's stride left hand IS the bass line of the piano; Peacock's bass IS the left hand of the ensemble.

garfield_thread.degree_0: Quincy Jones — attended late 1940s, class of 1950. Arranger/producer. Left Seattle.
garfield_thread.degree_3: Ernestine Anderson — attended late 1940s, same era as Jones. Vocalist. Left and returned.
garfield_thread.degree_4: Floyd Standifer — Central District post-WWII, educational path not confirmed as Garfield. Multi-instrumentalist and educator. Never left.
garfield_thread.degree_5: Overton Berry — Central District native, self-taught. No institutional music education. Autodidact path.
garfield_thread.degree_6: Patti Bown — Central District native, Seattle University (not Garfield). Formal training via Jesuit university.
garfield_thread.degree_7: Julian Priester — NOT a Garfield product. DuSable High School (Chicago) under Captain Dyett.
garfield_thread.degree_8: Gary Peacock — NOT a Garfield product. Burley, Idaho public schools. Westlake College of Music (LA). U.S. Army (Germany). The most remote origin of any S36 artist — rural Idaho, no connection to any major jazz pipeline until the Army.
